text: There is no one thing fit all answer for debugger. Different level of people responsible for different level of details. A debugger could easily expose overwhelming information for the wrong audience. It is important to tailor just-enough information to assist the task in hand.

elements.text: There is no one thing fit all answer for debugger. Different level of people responsible for different level of details. A debugger could easily expose overwhelming information for the wrong audience. It is important to tailor just-enough information to assist the task in hand.

text: "Going there" seems so 2019!  I always describe Dynamicland as trying to escape the gravitational pull of some of our established ways of collaborating.  In as much as they don't have an answer to reasonable, real-world questions, it's because they're searching for a new world, and they haven't arrived.  It's easy to assume that they want to sell us on a new, better world when, in fact, they are still flying through outer-space.

text:
Execution inspector.

I like <@U8A5MS6R1>’s proposal "slow motion replay" except that I am not aware of any debugger that actually works like that. Sometimes I'd love to be able to run a piece of code in slow motion, with various views on its inner state, rather than having to launch each step by hand.

text: This is really cool. That's all.

text:
```What does it take to get an idea from a research concept into practice?

...

for the sake of other would-be tech transfer pioneers... the next time they show you the new button they helped get put into Visual Studio or the approach that two people in a back office of Google are using instead of lamely saying "that's great", try flipping out and doing cartwheels down the hall. 
They have been part of a real accomplishment that is good for all of us as researchers, helping our profession stay relevant. Tech transfer is NOT just an implementation detail.```
Also, scratch for industrial robots!

<https://twitter.com/davidcshepherd/status/1380582222093148163>

attachments.fallback:
<https://twitter.com/davidcshepherd|@davidcshepherd>: What does it take to get an idea from a research concept into practice? Well, it's funny you should ask...

Please enjoy this series of videos that illustrate ONE idea's journey from concept to product. This work was done by a TON of different people, working as a team.
attachments.text:
What does it take to get an idea from a research concept into practice? Well, it's funny you should ask...

Please enjoy this series of videos that illustrate ONE idea's journey from concept to product. This work was done by a TON of different people, working as a team.

elements.text:
What does it take to get an idea from a research concept into practice?

...

for the sake of other would-be tech transfer pioneers... the next time they show you the new button they helped get put into Visual Studio or the approach that two people in a back office of Google are using instead of lamely saying "that's great", try flipping out and doing cartwheels down the hall. 
They have been part of a real accomplishment that is good for all of us as researchers, helping our profession stay relevant. Tech transfer is NOT just an implementation detail.

text: To walk step-by-step with your program, handholding through execution

text:
<@U01DX39SB33> Love the emphasis on declarative modeling and state machines - I think you guys sell yourselves short when you say it's for "reducing boilerplate". Lots of template/code generators exist that overemphasize ease of getting started, but end up delivering complex solutions when the app scales and people realize what they needed was better architecture, and not 2-days shaved off by skipping boilerplate. Software being hard *isn't* about mundane tasks like boilerplate, but rather our focus on easy solutions complecting together into something unmanageable.

What are the semantics around the infrastructure abstraction you guys have in Gadget? Seems like a lot of thought was put into scalability with Kubernetes and Cloud Functions (curious about the use of both, was there a reason why it couldn't be all serverless, or all K8s?), but given the product hasn't launched yet it's not clear how infrastructure is actually handled (terraform templates generated by gadget? custom code serving as the main engine, and cloud functions being the primary user code?). Would love some clarification on this side of the story
